preFix.title: Winterising an off-grid rainwater harvesting system
preFix.subtitle: Draining tanks, lagging exposed pipes, and protecting pumps before the first frost
preFix.excerpt: A rainwater harvesting system left unprotected through winter will crack pipes, damage pump seals, and split storage tanks if water freezes in them. Winterisation takes 2 to 3 hours and protects equipment that would be expensive to replace. This covers the sequence: when to start, what to drain, what to lag, and how to restore the system in spring.
preFix.sourceNotes: CIBSE TM61 rainwater harvesting system maintenance guidance. Rainwater Harvesting Association UK seasonal maintenance advice. RainWater Concepts UK installation and maintenance manuals.

content.text: An off-grid rainwater system typically consists of a storage tank (above-ground IBC, below-ground poly tank. Or surface-mounted butts), a first-flush diverter or silt trap, a pump or gravity outlet, and supply pipes. The parts most at risk of freeze damage are: exposed supply pipes, pump bodies, filter housings. And the first-flush diverter. Buried tanks are rarely at risk; frost rarely penetrates 600 mm in the UK. The work focuses on above-ground components.

In the UK, the risk of sustained frost begins in November in the north and December in the south. Start winterisation in mid-October to be safe. Do not wait for the first frost to be forecast. By then the work becomes urgent rather than planned. A system on an exposed site winterises 4 to 6 weeks earlier than a sheltered garden in a valley or urban area.
What to lag and what to drain

Lag: pipes that cannot be drained (e.g. a permanently connected supply line to an outbuilding or polytunnel) with 25 mm pipe lagging foam. Secure joints with waterproof tape. Lag any filter housing that will remain in place.
Drain: all removable pipe sections between the tank and outdoor taps. Disconnect at the tank outlet and tilt the pipe to drain, then store in a shed or garage.

Partially drain or top up: above-ground IBC tanks and butts should be partially drained to leave air space. A completely full plastic tank has no room to accommodate ice expansion at the surface. A tank with headspace copes with surface freezing without pressure damage.
Step-by-step instructions for Winterising an off-grid rainwater harvesting system go here.
